segments.teaching_goal: Learn to express surprise and contrast between expectation and reality.

narration_track.subtitle_en: This part of the sentence expresses a pleasant surprise. It starts with [zh:原来以为], meaning 'originally thought.' They thought [zh:时间会来不及], that there wouldn't be enough time. The key phrase here is [zh:没想到], which means 'unexpectedly' or 'didn't expect.' What was the surprise? [zh:学校很快就同意了] - 'the school agreed very quickly.' This structure is great for telling stories about surprising outcomes.

content.focus_text: 原来以为时间会来不及,没想到学校很快就同意了。
content.focus_pinyin: yuánlái yǐwéi shíjiān huì láibují, méi xiǎngdào xuéxiào hěn kuài jiù tóngyì le.
content.focus_gloss_en: They originally thought they wouldn't have enough time, but to their surprise, the school approved their application very quickly.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Indicates that it's too late to do something or there isn't sufficient time.
Literally 'didn't think of,' used to introduce a surprising event or outcome.
Used when someone gives their consent or approval.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn to describe a duration of time and express happiness.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Finally, we learn about the result of the application. [zh:两个人] means 'the two of them.' They can [zh:学习、生活] (study and live) in China for [zh:三、四个月] (three or four months). Notice how consecutive numbers are used to express an approximation. The last clause, [zh:这让他们非常高兴], explains their feelings. [zh:这] refers to the whole situation, [zh:让] means 'to make' or 'to let,' and [zh:非常高兴] means 'very happy.'

content.focus_text: 两个人可以在中国学习、生活三、四个月,这让他们非常高兴。
content.focus_pinyin: liǎng ge rén kěyǐ zài Zhōngguó xuéxí, shēnghuó sān, sì ge yuè, zhè ràng tāmen fēicháng gāoxìng.
content.focus_gloss_en: They can study and live in China for three or four months, which makes them very happy.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Can be used as both a verb (to live) and a noun (life).
Using consecutive numbers like this is a common way to indicate an approximate range.
A causative verb that indicates someone or something causes a certain result or feeling.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn to describe a sequence of travel using '先...然后...'.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Now, their journey begins. This sentence describes their travel plan using the structure [zh:先...然后...], which means 'first... then...'. First, [zh:先到上海], they arrive in Shanghai. Then, [zh:然后坐高铁去南京], they take the high-speed train to Nanjing. [zh:高铁] is the word for high-speed rail, a very common mode of transport in China.

content.focus_text: 张天明和丽莎先到上海,然后坐高铁去南京。
content.focus_pinyin: Zhāng Tiānmíng hé Lìshā xiān dào Shànghǎi, ránhòu zuò gāotiě qù Nánjīng.
content.focus_gloss_en: Zhang Tianming and Lisa first went to Shanghai, and then took the high-speed train to Nanjing.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A common structure to describe a sequence of actions.
Short for 高速铁路 (gāosù tiělù). A key part of modern China's infrastructure.
A common structure to describe a sequence of actions.

narration_track.subtitle_en: This sentence describes the train journey. [zh:车很快], 'the train was very fast.' How fast? [zh:一个多小时就到了]. [zh:一个多小时] means 'a little over an hour.' The word [zh:多] here indicates 'more than' or 'over.' The particle [zh:就] emphasizes that the arrival was quick or earlier than one might expect. It highlights the efficiency of the high-speed train.

content.focus_text: 车很快,一个多小时就到了。
content.focus_pinyin: Chē hěn kuài, yí ge duō xiǎoshí jiù dào le.
content.focus_gloss_en: The train was very fast, and they arrived in just over an hour.

highlight_words.explanation_en: When placed after a number and measure word, it indicates a quantity slightly more than that number.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn vocabulary related to meeting and greeting family.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Upon arrival, someone is there to meet them. [zh:天明的表哥], 'Tianming's older male cousin,' [zh:开车来接他们], 'drove to pick them up.' The verb [zh:接] means to pick someone up or to receive them. And [zh:表哥] is a specific term for a cousin, which we'll look at more closely.

content.focus_text: 天明的表哥开车来接他们。
content.focus_pinyin: Tiānmíng de biǎogē kāichē lái jiē tāmen.
content.focus_gloss_en: Tianming's cousin came to pick them up.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A male cousin from the mother's side or father's sister's side who is older than you.
A verb-object compound: 开 (to drive) + 车 (car).
Used for meeting and collecting someone from a location like an airport or train station.
A male cousin from the mother's side or father's sister's side who is older than you.

Used for meeting and collecting someone from a location like an airport or train station.

segments.teaching_goal: Understand the specific kinship term '表哥'.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Let's focus on the word [zh:表哥]. Chinese kinship terms can be very specific. The character [zh:表] indicates a cousin with a different surname, typically from your mother's side or your father's sister's side. [zh:哥] means 'older brother.' So, a [zh:表哥] is your older, male cousin from your maternal family or paternal aunts. This is different from cousins who share your surname.

content.focus_text: 表哥
content.focus_gloss_en: older male cousin (with a different surname)

highlight_words.explanation_en: In kinship terms, it refers to relatives with a different surname.
Used for one's own older brother or as a suffix for older male cousins.

content.notes: 表 (biǎo) denotes maternal cousins or paternal female cousins' children.

highlight_words.explanation_en: In kinship terms, it refers to relatives with a different surname.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn how to offer to show someone around.

narration_track.subtitle_en: The cousin is very welcoming. He was [zh:很高兴] (very happy) to see them and said he was [zh:愿意当导游], 'willing to be their tour guide.' He offers to [zh:带他们在街上走走], 'take them for a walk on the streets,' and [zh:让他们好好儿看看南京], 'let them have a good look at Nanjing.' The reduplicated verb [zh:走走] and [zh:看看] suggests a casual, relaxed action, and [zh:好好儿] means to do something thoroughly or to one's heart's content.

content.focus_text: 表哥见到他们很高兴,说愿意当导游,带他们在街上走走,让他们好好儿看看南京。
content.focus_pinyin: Biǎogē jiàndào tāmen hěn gāoxìng, shuō yuànyì dāng dǎoyóu, dài tāmen zài jiēshang zǒuzou, ràng tāmen hǎohāor kànkan Nánjīng.
content.focus_gloss_en: The cousin was very happy to see them and said he would be their guide and show them around Nanjing.

Refers to a public road in a city or town.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn to express a contrast between a past impression and present reality.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Here, Tianming expresses his surprise. He starts with a past impression from his father: [zh:爸爸常常说南京是个很安静的城市], 'Dad often said Nanjing was a very quiet city.' Then, he contrasts it with the present reality using [zh:没想到], 'unexpectedly.' The reality is that it's [zh:这么热闹], 'so bustling!' The words [zh:安静] (quiet) and [zh:热闹] (bustling, lively) are opposites, highlighting the dramatic change.

content.focus_text: 爸爸常常说南京是个很安静的城市,没想到这么热闹!
content.focus_pinyin: Bàba chángcháng shuō Nánjīng shì ge hěn ānjìng de chéngshì, méi xiǎngdào zhème rènao!
content.focus_gloss_en: Dad often said Nanjing was a very quiet city, but I didn't expect it to be so bustling!

Describes a place full of people, noise, and activity.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn to use '到处都' to describe something being everywhere.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Tianming continues describing the scene. He says [zh:你看], 'look,' to draw Lisa's attention. He uses the pattern [zh:到处都是] which means 'everywhere is...' or 'there are... everywhere.' What's everywhere? First, [zh:新盖的高楼], 'newly built tall buildings.' The verb [zh:盖] means 'to build.' Second, [zh:汽车], 'cars.' The repetition of [zh:到处都是] emphasizes the scale and density of the development.

content.focus_text: 你看,大街上到处都是新盖的高楼,到处都是汽车。
content.focus_pinyin: Nǐ kàn, dàjiē shang dàochù dōu shì xīn gài de gāolóu, dàochù dōu shì qìchē.
content.focus_gloss_en: Look, there are newly built tall buildings and cars everywhere on the main streets.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Used to indicate that something is present in all places.
Specifically used for constructing buildings.
高 (tall) + 楼 (building).

segments.teaching_goal: Understand how personal experience can illustrate broader societal changes.

narration_track.subtitle_en: The cousin confirms the changes with his own experience. He says [zh:这些年南京的变化是很大], 'Nanjing's changes in recent years have been huge.' He gives a concrete example: [zh:我以前骑自行车上班], 'I used to ride a bicycle to work.' [zh:现在也开起车来了], 'now I've also started driving a car.' This shift from [zh:自行车] (bicycle) to a car is a classic symbol of economic development and lifestyle change in China.

content.focus_text: 这些年南京的变化是很大,我以前骑自行车上班,现在也开起车来了。
content.focus_pinyin: Zhèxiē nián Nánjīng de biànhuà shì hěn dà, wǒ yǐqián qí zìxíngchē shàngbān, xiànzài yě kāi qǐ chē lái le.
content.focus_gloss_en: Nanjing has changed a lot in recent years. I used to ride a bike to work, but now I've started driving a car too.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Can be a noun or a verb, referring to transformation.
Used for riding things you straddle, like a bike, horse, or motorcycle.
A verb-object phrase: 上 (to go to) + 班 (work shift).

segments.teaching_goal: Distinguish between the verb '变' and the noun/verb '变化'.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Let's clarify the difference between [zh:变] and [zh:变化]. While both relate to change, their grammar is different. [zh:变] is a verb that can take an object, meaning 'to change into something.' For example, [zh:中学变成了一个购物中心], 'The middle school turned into a shopping center.' On the other hand, [zh:变化] is often used as a noun, as in our text: [zh:南京的变化很大], 'Nanjing's changes are big.' It can also be a verb, but it doesn't take an object, like [zh:天气变化得很快], 'The weather changed quickly.'

highlight_words.explanation_en: A transitive verb, often followed by 成 (chéng).
Can be a noun (a change) or an intransitive verb (to undergo change).

segments.teaching_goal: Learn to express that something is different from one's imagination.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Lisa shares her impression. She says, [zh:这跟我以前想像的中国完全不一样]. This means, 'This is completely different from the China I had imagined before.' The structure is 'A [zh:跟] B [zh:不一样]' meaning 'A is not the same as B.' The key verb here is [zh:想像], 'to imagine.' The adverb [zh:完全] means 'completely,' emphasizing the degree of difference.

content.focus_text: 这跟我以前想像的中国完全不一样。
content.focus_pinyin: Zhè gēn wǒ yǐqián xiǎngxiàng de Zhōngguó wánquán bù yīyàng.
content.focus_gloss_en: This is completely different from the China I had imagined.

highlight_words.explanation_en: 想 (to think) + 象 (image).
An adverb used to add emphasis.
The negative form of 一样 (yīyàng, same).

segments.teaching_goal: Learn the hypothetical structure '要不是...' (if it were not for...).

narration_track.subtitle_en: Lisa elaborates on her feeling about Shanghai. She uses the structure [zh:要不是...我还以为...], which means 'If it weren't for... I would have thought...' She says, [zh:要不是到处都是中文], 'if it weren't for the Chinese characters everywhere,' [zh:我还以为是在美国呢], 'I would have thought I was in America.' This is a great way to make a strong comparison by stating a counterfactual condition.

content.focus_text: 要不是到处都是中文,我还以为是在美国呢。
content.focus_pinyin: yàobúshì dàochù dōu shì Zhōngwén, wǒ hái yǐwéi shì zài Měiguó ne.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Introduces a hypothetical condition that is contrary to the facts.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn to contrast the past and present regarding the presence of foreigners.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Tianming adds another point of change, this time from his father's memory. [zh:他小时候], 'when he was little,' he [zh:很少看到外国人], 'rarely saw foreigners.' The word [zh:可是] introduces a contrast, meaning 'but.' [zh:可是现在], 'but now,' [zh:到处都有外国游客啊], 'there are foreign tourists everywhere!' The final particle [zh:啊] adds a tone of exclamation and emphasis, highlighting the surprise and scale of the change.

content.focus_text: 我爸爸说,他小时候在南京很少看到外国人,可是现在,你看,到处都有外国游客啊!
content.focus_pinyin: Wǒ bàba shuō, tā xiǎo shíhou zài Nánjīng hěn shǎo kàndào wàiguórén, kěshì xiànzài, nǐ kàn, dàochù dōu yǒu wàiguó yóukè a!
content.focus_gloss_en: My dad said that when he was little, he rarely saw foreigners in Nanjing, but now, as you can see, there are foreign tourists everywhere!

narration_track.subtitle_en: Lisa makes a humorous point. She asks a rhetorical question: [zh:我们不也是两个“老外”吗]? 'Aren't we two 'foreigners' as well?' [zh:老外] is a very common and generally neutral colloquial term for 'foreigner.' It's less formal than [zh:外国人]. The structure '不也...吗?' is used to make a point emphatically, implying 'we are also...'

content.focus_text: 我们不也是两个“老外”吗?哈哈!
content.focus_pinyin: Wǒmen bù yě shì liǎng ge “lǎowài” ma? Hāhā!

narration_track.subtitle_en: Tianming offers a positive perspective on these changes. He says, [zh:中国融入世界了]. This means 'China has integrated into the world.' content.focus_text: 中国融入世界了,这是件好事。

segments.teaching_goal: Learn to express worry and concern about a future trend.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Lisa, however, expresses a concern. She starts with [zh:可是我担心], 'But I'm worried.' Her worry is about the future: [zh:这样下去], 'if this goes on.' The question she poses is, [zh:有中国特色的东西会不会越来越少呢]? 'Will there be fewer and fewer things with Chinese characteristics?' [zh:特色] means 'unique characteristics,' and [zh:越来越少] means 'less and less.' This introduces the central debate of the lesson: development versus tradition.

segments.teaching_goal: Understand the use of '下去' to indicate continuation.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Let's look at the phrase [zh:这样下去]. The word [zh:下去] here is a directional complement, but its meaning is abstract. When placed after a verb or an adjective, it suggests the continuation of an action or a state. For example, 'to continue speaking' is [zh:说下去]. In our text, [zh:这样下去] means 'if it continues like this' or 'if this state persists.' It's a useful way to talk about the future consequences of a current trend.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand how to express that a place has kept its traditional features despite modernization.

The word [zh:特色] means 'characteristic' or 'distinguishing feature,' so [zh:中国特色] refers to things that are uniquely Chinese. This reassures the listener that not everything has become Westernized.

content.focus_gloss_en: No. Nanjing has still preserved many things with Chinese characteristics.

[zh:吃的] means 'things to eat,' [zh:喝的] means 'things to drink,' and [zh:玩儿的] means 'things to do for fun' or 'amusements.' segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to make a suggestion to go somewhere and try something.

narration_track.subtitle_en: This is a direct suggestion. It starts with [zh:那], meaning 'then' or 'in that case.' The speaker suggests, 'let's go to the Temple of Confucius now,' [zh:我们现在就去夫子庙]. The goal is to [zh:尝尝南京小吃], 'try some Nanjing snacks.' Notice the verb reduplication in [zh:尝尝], which softens the tone and implies trying something out casually. The particle [zh:吧] at the end also reinforces the suggestion.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand the '不管...都/也...' structure for expressing 'no matter what'.

narration_track.subtitle_en: The speaker agrees enthusiastically with [zh:好啊]. Then, they use the structure 'no matter what,' which is [zh:不管]. They say, 'no matter how China changes, no matter how Nanjing changes,' [zh:不管中国怎么变, 南京怎么变]. The one thing that hasn't changed, [zh:没变], is that 'people have to eat when they're hungry,' [zh:肚子饿了要吃]. This is a philosophical but humorous observation about a fundamental human need.

content.focus_text: 好啊!不管中国怎么变,南京怎么变,肚子饿了要吃没变。

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to ask if someone has heard of a particular saying.

narration_track.subtitle_en: The speaker then introduces a famous Chinese saying. They ask, 'Have you heard of...?' using the phrase [zh:你们听说过...吗]. The saying is [zh:民以食为天]. segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to express confusion and state that you've never heard something before.

narration_track.subtitle_en: This is a very natural and common response when you don't understand something. The speaker starts with [zh:啊], expressing surprise, followed by [zh:什么] for 'What?'. They then state clearly, [zh:从来没听说过], which means 'I've never heard of it.' The word [zh:从来] means 'from the past until the present' and is often used with a negative like [zh:没] or [zh:不] to mean 'never'.

He uses the [zh:把] structure, which brings the object forward to emphasize what is being done to it. What is the object? [zh:今天拍的照片], 'the photos taken today'. He will [zh:发给他], 'send them to him', so his dad can [zh:看看], 'have a look'. Reduplicating the verb '看' softens the tone and suggests a casual look.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand how to express contrast with '可是' and use '又...又...' to describe concurrent states.

narration_track.subtitle_en: The speaker, who is the tour guide, explains why he's hungry. He starts with [zh:走了这么多路], 'We've walked so much.' He observes that the others, [zh:你们], 'you all', [zh:好像不累不饿], 'seem not tired and not hungry'. Then comes the contrast with [zh:可是], 'but'. He, the [zh:导游] or 'tour guide', is [zh:又渴又饿]. This 'both...and...' structure, [zh:又...又...], is used to link two adjectives describing the same subject. He is both thirsty, [zh:渴], and hungry, [zh:饿].

segments.teaching_goal: To learn a famous Chinese idiom about the importance of food and how to make a suggestion.

narration_track.subtitle_en: The guide concludes with a powerful statement. He starts with [zh:别忘了], 'Don't forget'. Then comes the famous idiom: [zh:民以食为天]. This literally means 'the people take food as heaven,' emphasizing that for ordinary people, food is the most important thing. It's a cornerstone of Chinese culture. The particle [zh:啊] adds emphasis. Finally, he makes a clear suggestion: [zh:走,吃饭去], 'Let's go, let's go eat!' It's a direct and energetic call to action.

content.focus_text: 别忘了,“民以食为天”啊!走,吃饭去!

segments.teaching_goal: To understand and use the modal particle '啊' to express various emotions and soften the tone of a sentence.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Now let's focus on a very common particle in spoken Chinese: [zh:啊]. You'll hear it at the end of sentences all the time. Its main job is to add emotional color. First, it can heighten excitement or exclamation, like in the sentence [zh:到处都有外国游客啊], meaning 'there are foreign tourists everywhere!'. It can also be used in a declarative sentence to remind or explain something, as when the cousin says [zh:那就是夫子庙啊], 'That is the Temple of Confucius.' Another key function is to soften the tone of a question or a command, making it sound more gentle or casual. For example, you could ask [zh:我们什么时候走啊], 'When are we leaving?'. Notice that the sound of [zh:啊] can change depending on the final sound of the word before it, sometimes sounding like 'wa', 'na', or 'ya' for smoother pronunciation.

content.focus_text: Q: 我们什么时候走啊? A: 好啊!
content.focus_pinyin: Wǒmen shénme shíhou zǒu a? Hǎo a!
content.focus_gloss_en: Q: When are we leaving? A: Great!

grammar_points.explanation_en: Used in exclamatory sentences to heighten emotion. Example: 到处都有外国游客啊! (There are foreign tourists everywhere!)
Used in declarative sentences to explain or remind. Example: 那就是夫子庙啊。 (That is the Temple of Confucius.)
Used at the end of a question to soften the tone. Example: 你怎么不说话呀? (How come you aren't saying anything?)
Used to tone down an imperative sentence. Example: 小心点儿啊! (Be careful!)

segments.segment_title: The Structure 以 X 为 Y
segments.teaching_goal: To learn the formal structure '以 X 为 Y' (yǐ X wéi Y), meaning 'to regard X as Y'.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Next, let's look at a more formal, literary structure: [zh:以 X 为 Y]. This pattern means 'to regard X as Y' or 'to treat X as Y'. It's constructed by taking an object X, and considering it as Y. A crucial pronunciation point is that the character [zh:为] here is a verb, pronounced in the second tone, [zh:为]. The most famous example, which you heard in the dialogue, is [zh:民以食为天]. This breaks down to '[zh:民]' (the people) '[zh:以]' (take) '[zh:食]' (food) '[zh:为]' (as) '[zh:天]' (heaven or the sky). So, it means 'The people regard food as the most important thing.' This structure adds a classic and formal touch to your Chinese.

content.focus_text: 民以食为天。
content.focus_pinyin: Mín yǐ shí wéi tiān.
content.focus_gloss_en: Ordinary people consider food as important as heaven.

grammar_points.explanation_en: A formal structure meaning 'to regard or treat X as Y'.

segments.teaching_goal: To use the '一 + Reduplicated Measure Word' structure to describe a large number of similar items.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Let's examine a descriptive structure: 'one + a reduplicated measure word'. You saw this in the dialogue with [zh:一栋栋高楼大厦] and [zh:一座座传统建筑]. This pattern is used to describe a large group of similar, yet individual, objects. It creates a vivid image of 'one after another'. For example, [zh:一栋栋] paints a picture of many tall buildings, one next to the other. Similarly, [zh:一座座] suggests numerous traditional buildings. This is more descriptive than simply saying [zh:很多高楼] (many tall buildings). It emphasizes the quantity and the visual repetition of the items. Another example would be [zh:一盘盘水果], meaning 'plate after plate of fruit'.

content.focus_text: 南京一边是一栋栋高楼大厦, 一边是一座座传统建筑。
content.focus_pinyin: Nánjīng yībiān shì yī dòng dòng gāolóu dàshà, yībiān shì yī zuò zuò chuántǒng jiànzhù.

segments.teaching_goal: To review the key vocabulary and grammar patterns from the lesson on 'Changes in China'.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Great job! In this lesson, we discussed the many changes in China. We learned how to express surprise with [zh:没想到], talk about past assumptions with [zh:原来以为], and describe things happening 'everywhere' with [zh:到处]. We also covered key grammar like the particle [zh:啊], the structure [zh:以...为...], and reduplicated measure words. Review these key phrases and try to use them to talk about changes in your own city.
